metadata.description: Held-out negotiation rapport_building pairs for probing only. NOT used in vector extraction. Same domain (negotiation) as extraction pairs but entirely new scenarios. Tests whether the rapport_building vector generalises within negotiation contexts.

dimensions.name: Rapport Building
dimensions.description: Establishing trust, connection, and a positive relationship with the counterpart; going beyond purely transactional interaction

pairs.context: You are meeting a new client for the first time before the negotiation begins.
pairs.positive: It's great to finally meet you in person. I noticed you went to Edinburgh — I spent some time there. Did you stay after graduation or head straight to London?
pairs.negative: Good to meet you. Should we get into the agenda? I've put together a few talking points on the deal structure.

pairs.context: At the start of a call with a long-term supplier, you notice they mentioned their company had a big week.
pairs.positive: I saw the news about your new contract — congratulations. That's a significant win. How's the team feeling about it?
pairs.negative: Good, let's get started. I wanted to go through a couple of pricing issues on the current contract.

pairs.context: A client mentions in passing that they've just returned from a family holiday.
pairs.positive: Welcome back — where did you go? I hope you managed to properly switch off before we dragged you back into work conversations.
pairs.negative: Good, you're back. I've been waiting on a couple of decisions that need your sign-off. When can we pick those up?

pairs.context: You are in a negotiation that has been tense; there is a natural pause before resuming.
pairs.positive: Let's take a minute before we continue. This has been a pretty intense morning. How are you doing with all of this?
pairs.negative: Okay, so where we got to was the indemnity clause. Shall we pick that back up?

pairs.context: You are about to start a difficult renegotiation with a long-standing partner.
pairs.positive: Before we get into the numbers, I want to say that the relationship we've built over the past four years matters to me. I want this conversation to be difficult in a productive way, not in a way that damages what we have.
pairs.negative: So we need to talk about the pricing structure. Some things have changed on our side and the current terms don't work the way they used to.

pairs.context: A new counterpart makes a self-deprecating comment about being new to this type of negotiation.
pairs.positive: Don't worry about it — everyone is new to something. I'd rather have an honest conversation with someone who says they're still learning than a slick one with someone who pretends they know everything.
pairs.negative: That's fine. We'll walk through the key points and you can ask questions as we go.

pairs.context: You notice that your counterpart seems stressed and distracted at the start of a meeting.
pairs.positive: You seem like you've got a lot on today. We don't have to rush through this — do you want to take a few minutes before we start, or reschedule if today isn't the right time?
pairs.negative: We've both got busy schedules so let's try to get through the main items efficiently.

pairs.context: A potential partner mentions they share a mutual contact with you.
pairs.positive: Oh, you know Marcus? He's someone I've worked with for years — genuinely one of the best people in the field. How do you know him?
pairs.negative: Small world. Shall we get into the partnership terms? I've got a draft I'd like to walk you through.

pairs.context: At the end of a successful negotiation, the other party seems relieved.
pairs.positive: I'm glad we got there. That was a genuinely hard conversation in places and I appreciate how you handled it. I think we've set ourselves up well.
pairs.negative: Good. I'll have legal draft the agreement and send it over for review. Expect it by end of week.

pairs.context: A counterpart mentions they've been dealing with internal politics that have complicated their side of the negotiation.
pairs.positive: That sounds genuinely frustrating. Trying to negotiate externally while managing internal disagreements at the same time is really draining. I appreciate you being upfront about it.
pairs.negative: I understand there are complications on your side. It would help me to know if any of those affect the decisions you're able to make here.

pairs.context: You are starting a negotiation with someone you've not met before but have corresponded with at length over email.
pairs.positive: It's good to finally meet you properly. After all those emails, it's strange to realise we've never actually spoken. I appreciate how thoroughly you've engaged with the documents we've sent back and forth.
pairs.negative: So we've been going back and forth on this for a while. I'd like to try to resolve a few of the outstanding issues today.

pairs.context: A counterpart references a previous negotiation that went badly for them and sounds wary.
pairs.positive: That sounds like a difficult experience. I can understand why you'd be cautious coming into another conversation like this one. I'd like to do this differently — would it help to talk about how we're going to approach it before we get into content?
pairs.negative: I can assure you we're a professional outfit. Shall we get into the specifics of the current proposal?

pairs.context: You're starting a day-long negotiation session and both sides are meeting for breakfast before it begins.
pairs.positive: I'm glad we did this before jumping in. I always find these conversations go better when both sides feel like people rather than positions. What gets you into work on something like this?
pairs.negative: Good to meet you all. The main session starts at 9 — is there anything you want to flag before we go in?

pairs.context: A counterpart compliments the quality of the proposal you submitted.
pairs.positive: Thank you — that genuinely means something to me. We put a lot into making it something you'd actually want to read. I'm glad it landed that way.
pairs.negative: Thank you. I'm happy to walk you through any of the sections in more detail.

pairs.context: A long-term client has just renewed a contract that was at risk.
pairs.positive: I'm really pleased we got this done. We've been through some difficult moments over the past year and I'd rather have you as a partner on the other side of that than not. Thank you for sticking with it.
pairs.negative: Great. I'll confirm the new terms in writing and we can get the contract signed off.
